proposal_text:
Proposal number: 1333885

Proposal: LET'S WALK! - climate change awareness day event

Summary


Purpose: helping to rise the climate change awareness our world so urgently needs. 

Suggested actions: During that day we encourage people to walk and to:

leave their cars at home,
skip the bus,
avoid the subway and
turn to their own bodies in order to move around the city, by walking, by using their bicycles, their rollers, their skates.
If you are not lucky enough to be living near your work space you can still collaborate by doing home office and other activities such as walking to the grocery or the club.
Using a smartphone app we intend to track how much people walk during the day of the event. This information will then be published by social media and traditional media. The data provided by the app will also be translate in the reductions already mentioned, calculating savings in relationship  with the kilometrers walked and avoiding using transportation during that day.The application may use a similar alogorithm that "calculate your ecological footprint" uses, that is available at the Government web page [14].
Because of the altruistic spirit of the cause, we expect to have support from the local ONG´s, health organizations and the government.     
Main value generation loops v1.0.png


Short term Impacts: we expect to save energy and money, GHG emissions, and we will contribute to build sustainable habits, for a healthy environment in the war against the dangers of a sedentary lifestyle.  

Long term impacts:

Since the proposal is to make people aware of how important it is to care about the planet. More climate change aware people would naturally support the following initiatives:

Recycling: We forecast that it could increase by 10 %,
 
Energy savings: recommendations use increment by 10 %
 
Distributed solar photovoltaic electricity law: is approved and covers 5 % of city demand.
 
Electric buses: their use is implemented in 2 % of city bus lines.
Is this proposal for a practice or a project?
Practice

What actions do you propose?
a) The main thrust of “Let’s Walk” is walking, because Buenos Aires has a high population density, and distances might be shorter than in other cities, so walking to work is a real possibility for some people. People can

Walk 10 - 15 blocks to their offices or back home.

Use public transportation, including buses, underground / subways and then walk the rest of the way to their offices or homes.

 

b) Cycling also adds value to the “Let’s walk” proposal for the population 10 to 30 blocks from their office. The Buenos Aires City already has implemented an “ecobici” (eco bikes) program [15]. The ecobici system has bikes parked in several distributed city points, and anyone can use one of these bikes for free using his / her document. You just return the bike in another bike parking place near your destination.

 

REVIEW OF SOME “BIKE TO WORK” PROGRAMS IN CANADA AND USA:

USA Today informs that Bike to Work programs might be rising in US [21].

Through a google search we found the following relevant links:

table-bike-to-work-projects.png


ANSWERS TO SOME OBJECTIONS TO “BIKE TO WORK”

The Huffpost article [16] mentions 9 objections to “Bike to work”, here are our thoughts about them!

main-objections.png

“LET’S WALK BUSINESS MODEL CANVAS”

LET`S WALK PROJECT BUSINESS CANVAS v2.0.png

As we know we have different partners in this event, which play a fundamental role.

On one side, the people enrolled in the event, that will participate registering using a mobile app. They will either walk to their works or commute by bike. In order to be eligible to receive a prize, each participant will allow the app to register information about distances walked / biked, which transportation they usually use to commute in order to calculate GHG emission savings due to the event. Those people that contribute with the biggest GHG emission savings will win prizes as well as  benefits from companies that sponsor the event.

It´s necessary to distinguish, that by incentivizing the movement we are preventing obesity, decrease bad cholesterol and increase good cholesterol, improving the state of mind and incorporating healthy habits, so typically HMOs and the city government will be very much interested, as in Argentina poor people use public hospitals maintained by city taxes.

On the other side, we need to be able to organize this event with which we need the Government's support to convey the event to the society. It will be benefited by an increased use of the public transport by the population, better flow of the traffic (less cars in the streets)  and at the same time it will reaffirm it´s commitment with the environment.

In addition to this, it will be a great opportunity to advertise the use of the eco-bikes(“ecobicis”) [15] and, as a result, the bike lanes.

On the other hand, the information generated by the app (big data)  will be of great utility since it will show which are the population's needs (for example: the best locations for entrances of subways , routes of buses or even which are the areas that are disconnected and need public transport).

It is also important to have the companies that will join on this great event allowing the use of “home office”. In addition, they will collaborate as sponsors and obtain in exchange Big Data to improve its business and find niche.

Sponsorships are one of the best ways of promoting a business and winning popularity between the target and generate good marketing for a brand. It can be specially used to increase credibility, image and prestige of the brand.

All participation is voluntary, and public transportation will still be available to those who want to use it.

LGC Team will ask for suggestions and advice from NGOs(such as WWF, who implemented "The earth hour"). In order to have more tools to guide the team on how to develop the project. 
 
Once LCG Team recieves feedback from NGOs.They will present the formal proposal to the Institute of Technology of Buenos Aires (ITBA) and to the MIT Colab
 
The team is looking forward to receive feedback from CLIMATE COLAB in order to improve the project. ITBA will give their consent and support to it.
 
We received the recognition and prize from MIT Colab thanks to the backing through votes from ITBA.
 
LGC Team will contact several organizations to help with fundraising and to add value to the final event (GOOGLE, MASS MEDIA, SPONSORS, etc). Nevertheless to make it happen we need firstly the GOVERNMENT authorization.
 
The government will notificate the companies of the proposal and ask for their cooperation by allowing the employees to work from home. They will also have to arrange with the transportation about the modification of their routes.
 
Distance walked and GHG savings: To have an estimation of the GHGs saved is necessary an app (develop by GOOGLE if possible) that will measure them.The application may use a similar alogorithm that "calculate your ecological footprint" uses, that is available at the Government web page.But it is important to keep record of the route like Google Maps does. 
 
Once the proposal is accepted the next step is to settle a date and communicate it to the society, a key actor in this process is MASS MEDIA. 
 
The day of the event SPONSORS will play an important role by motivating the population to walk more. This could be achived by offering refreshments and prizes. 
 
Finally,all the information is collected by GOOGLE. Including steps per person and the equivalence with the reduction of GHGs obteined.
 
The LGC Team will analyze the results and make papers to be published and to communicate it to the community, the government and the MIT. 
 
As the results will be positive this idea can be implemented in other countries, all over the world. By this the proposal will reach a greater impact and a change in people's habits worldwide.


Who will take these actions?
LGC Team at ITBA: designs proposal and presents it to Climate Colab.
 
Argentine Government sponsors "LET'S WALK day"
 
Media covers the events publishing metrics on energy saved, emissions saved and money saved.
 
5 % local city population participates in the "LET'S WALK day"
 
NGOs sponsor event.
 
Transportation works as usual.

Other countries can implement the proposal to have a higher impact
Where will these actions be taken?
City level: The first objective is to do it in Buenos Aires City as is the most populated region, the most contaminating one and the principal concentration of companies.

Country level: Later by the positive results our goal is to carry out the experience at country level, in cities like Cordoba, Rosario, Mendoza and San Luis.

Worldwide: Finally, other countries can use the proposal to make their own impacts since everything counts.

 

In addition, specify the country or countries where these actions will be taken.
Argentina

Country 2
Brazil

Country 3
Chile

Country 4
United States

Country 5
No country selected

Impact/Benefits
What impact will these actions have on greenhouse gas emissions and/or adapting to climate change?
1) City of Buenos Aires

a) Short term CO2e emissions saved in Buenos Aires city:

We postulate that 5 % of the population will participate in the event.
Public transportation use: 80% of population [1]

 
Emissions saved by cars commuting from Buenos Aires Province:

10410352 cars/month [2] /30 days/month x 15 km/trip  x 0.1 kg CO2eq / km [3] x 0.05 ( 5 % population participates  in event) = 26 tCO2e (metric tons) / day.
 

Emission saved by cars inside the city:

2.89 million people x 5% people participation x 20 % people use cars x 7 km average trip to work x 2 trips /day x 0.1 kg CO2eq / km [3] = 40.46 tCO2e / day
 

Total short term CO2e savings = (26 + 40.46) = 66.46 tCO2e /day

b) Long term CHG savings (5 years) in Buenos Aires city:

We forecast that recycling could increase by 10 %. Today only 23% of Buenos Aires city recycle [4]. And recycling saves from 0.34 to 10 Tonnes of CO2e from each ton of recycled material [5]. Considering an average 1 Tonne of CO2e saved per Tonne of recycled material, current Buenos Aires savings from recycling would be 976 Tonnes of domiciliary residues [6] x 23% people recycle x 0.1 (10 % recycling increase ) x 1 tCO2e/1 t recycled material = 22 Tonnes of CO2e / year saved.
 
Energy savings recommendations [7] use increment by 10 %. The average electricity consumption per electrified household in Argentina is 3600 kWh [11], so we estimate that the savings will be of 360 kWh/home per year * 963 thousand homes * 0.05 (5 % of participation) = 17334 kWh per year. This could save up to 17334 kWh x 0.0005925 tCO2e/kWh = 10.2 tCO2e/year
Distributed solar photo voltaic electricity law is approved and covers 1 % of city demand.
2.89 million people x 4100 kWh/person [10] x 0.01 (1% of demand) x 0.0005925 tCO2e/kWh = 70205 tCO2e/year
 
It incentivates the use of public transport in this way, so the Government must make it more efficient.For example, the use of electric buses is implemented in 2 % of city.[8] [9] Each bus will save 175 metrics tones of pollutant emissions and 22.700 liters of fuel per unit and year. We estimate that there are 10200 buses in Buenos Aires,so 2% is 204, the total reduction will be 204*175 mtones= 35700 metric tones of pollutant / year saved.[10]
 
Total emission savings = (22 + 10.2 + 70205 + 35700) = 105937 tCO2e/year

2) Extrapolation to world:

a) Short term:

66.46 tCO2e/(2.89million people) x (1087 million people) = 24997 tCO2e

b) Long term:

105.937 tCO2e/year /(2.89million people) x (1087 million people) = 39 million tCO2e / year.

The transportation sector accounts from 19 % to 25 % of total GHGs emissions, according to [12] and [13]

So the total GHG due to the transportation sector may be calculated as

46.000 million tCO2e x 22% = 10.120 million tCO2e

So the proposal savings would be 39 / 10120 = 0.38 % of the transportation sector emissions.

What are other key benefits?
Generate awareness on using more public transport
Government will have to improve the efficiency of public transport
Change the habits of the society towards a sustainable and ecological life
Smog and sound pollution will be reduced
People´s stress levels will be reduced
Increase the use of bicycles
Change habits and weaken sedentary lifestyle
Cut costs of transport and so combustibles
By implemeting home-office besides helping this cause, the employeers will have a cut down of their costs
Decrease GHGs emissions by reducing the use of cars and public transport
Costs/Challenges
What are the proposal’s projected costs?
1) Cost of security forces involved in organizing the event.

2) Cost of health support personnel, ambulances, paramedics,

3) Cost of traditional media ads

4) Cost of blog, SN ads and  social media managers / influencers

5) Cost of winner prizes

6) Cost of collaboration agreement with smartphone apps that calculate distances walked by people participating in the event

7) Cost of webpage that summarizes energy savings, money saved, GHGs emission saved.

Timeline
The day of the event, there will be an immediate impact by the reduction of the GHGs emissions.
In the short-term we are looking forward to make people realize that their can make a huge change on a daily basis.For example, they can avoid the use of cars in short distances.
Over more, we can affirm that one of important change will be the use bikes. Nowadays, Buenos Aires city provides free bikes and bicicles in the downtown. Our idea is to massify these kind of habits and became a green country free of sedentarism.
What's more, we want to Incentive companies to stablish a home-office day. This will allow people who live far from their jobs to colaborate to this cause.
Medium-term: These "Homeoffice days" could at first be once a year, but once people catch the habit, it could be done monthly.
Long-term: Eventually people could do these "Homeoffice days" weekly, so that people enjoy life a little more and savings of energy, money and GHGs emissions are multiplied.

feasibility.rationale: The proposal relies on a highly optimistic and unverified assumption that 5% of the city's population (approx. 145,000 people) will voluntarily participate in a single-day event without significant logistical friction. Furthermore, the technical feasibility of the proposed 'Google-developed' app is speculative; the proposal assumes a major tech corporation will build custom infrastructure for a one-off event without a clear business case or prior commitment. The requirement for government authorization to 'modify bus routes' for a single day is operationally complex and unlikely to be approved for a temporary awareness event.

novelty.rationale: The core concept—a 'Walk to Work' or 'Bike to Work' day—is a well-established global practice (e.g., Earth Hour, Bike to Work Day) with extensive existing literature. While the proposal attempts to integrate a specific data-tracking app and local context (Buenos Aires), the fundamental mechanism of action is not novel. The proposed algorithm for calculating footprints is explicitly stated to be a replication of existing government tools, offering no new scientific or technical methodology.

impact.rationale: The short-term impact calculation (66.46 tCO2e/day) is mathematically plausible for a single day but represents a negligible fraction of annual emissions. The long-term impact projections are scientifically unsound due to a lack of causal evidence. The proposal asserts a direct causal link between a one-day walking event and a 10% increase in recycling, the approval of solar laws, and the implementation of electric buses. These are systemic policy outcomes that cannot be reliably attributed to a single behavioral awareness event; the correlation is assumed without empirical support, leading to a massive overestimation of impact (e.g., projecting 39 million tCO2e/year globally from a single city's pilot).

presentation.rationale: The presentation suffers from significant structural and logical inconsistencies. The text oscillates between a 'practice' and a 'project' without clarity. The impact section contains severe logical leaps, attributing long-term infrastructure and policy changes (solar laws, electric bus fleets) directly to the event's awareness campaign. The reliance on hypothetical partnerships (Google, MIT Colab) and the assumption that the government will modify public transport routes for a single day undermines the credibility of the technical and operational narrative.

A1.perspective_summary: From a systems and empirical evidence perspective, this proposal conflates a short-term behavioral nudge with long-term systemic policy shifts. While the immediate emission savings from a single day of reduced car use are calculable, the causal chain linking this event to major infrastructure changes (solar laws, electric bus adoption) and global scaling is scientifically unsupported. The proposal lacks a robust mechanism for the claimed long-term habit formation and relies on speculative partnerships for its technical execution.
key_concerns: Causal Overreach: The proposal assumes a one-day event will directly cause complex policy outcomes (solar law approval, electric bus implementation) without evidence of such a causal mechanism.
Data Integrity: The methodology for tracking and verifying GHG savings relies on a hypothetical app and unverified user self-reporting, introducing high uncertainty into the impact metrics.

Operational Feasibility: The assumption that 5% of the population will participate and that public transport routes will be modified for a single day is operationally unrealistic without a detailed implementation plan.
Scaling Logic: The extrapolation from a single city event to global impact (39 million tCO2e/year) ignores the diminishing returns and contextual differences of scaling behavioral interventions.
Dependency Risk: The proposal's technical execution is contingent on the voluntary cooperation of major entities (Google, Government) that have not been secured.

feasibility.rationale: The proposal relies on unrealistic institutional levers, specifically the assumption that the government can mandate companies to allow 'home office' and that transportation authorities will modify routes based on a single-day event. The governance model assumes voluntary participation will yield 5% of the city's population (approx. 145,000 people) without a clear enforcement or incentive mechanism beyond vague 'prizes.' Furthermore, the reliance on a Google-developed app and the assumption that the government will 'notify' companies of the proposal ignores the complex regulatory and labor frameworks required to shift work habits at scale.

novelty.rationale: The concept of 'Walk to Work' or 'Bike to Work' days is a well-established global practice (e.g., Earth Hour, Bike to Work Day in the US/Canada). The proposal's novelty lies primarily in the specific integration of a tracking app to generate 'big data' for urban planning and the attempt to link this data to long-term policy outcomes (solar law, electric buses). However, the core mechanism is not innovative, and the proposal does not address how this specific iteration differs significantly from existing, often more mature, municipal programs.

impact.rationale: While the short-term emission savings (66 tCO2e/day) are mathematically derived, the long-term impact projections are highly speculative and lack a causal governance link. The proposal forecasts a 10% increase in recycling and the approval of a distributed solar law as direct results of a single walking event, which is a significant overestimation of behavioral spillover. The governance capacity to translate a one-day awareness event into structural changes like '2% electric bus implementation' or 'solar law approval' is not demonstrated, making the long-term impact claims unrealistic.

presentation.rationale: The proposal is structured with clear sections (Purpose, Actions, Impacts, Costs) and attempts to use data to support claims. However, the presentation suffers from logical gaps in the policy argumentation, such as the jump from a walking event to the approval of specific legislation. The text also contains grammatical errors and inconsistent formatting (e.g., image placeholders like 'main-objections.png' without context), which undermines the professional credibility required for a governance-focused proposal.

A2.perspective_summary: From a governance and regulatory perspective, this proposal is an awareness campaign masquerading as a policy intervention. It lacks the institutional depth to enforce the behavioral changes it predicts and relies on optimistic assumptions about government and corporate cooperation that are not grounded in current regulatory realities. While the data collection idea has merit, the proposal fails to outline the necessary political or administrative levers to achieve its ambitious long-term policy goals.
key_concerns: The proposal assumes the government can mandate or strongly influence corporate 'home office' policies without a legal framework or labor agreement.
There is no clear mechanism for how a one-day event translates into long-term legislative outcomes (e.g., solar law approval, electric bus adoption).
The reliance on a single-day event to shift deep-seated commuting habits ignores the need for sustained infrastructure investment and policy continuity.
The data privacy and governance implications of a government-linked app tracking citizen movement are not addressed.
The assumption that 5% of the population will voluntarily participate without significant incentives or mandates is unproven.

feasibility.rationale: The proposal relies on a 'big bang' single-day event model with high coordination costs (security, health, media) but lacks a clear operational plan for the critical dependency on government authorization and corporate buy-in for home-office policies. The assumption that 5% of the population will voluntarily walk to work on a specific day without a robust incentive structure or logistical support (e.g., traffic management, safety) is operationally optimistic. Furthermore, the reliance on a custom app developed by 'Google if possible' without a defined technical partner or budget creates a significant execution risk.

novelty.rationale: The core concept of 'Walk to Work' or 'Bike to Work' days is a well-established global practice (referenced in the proposal itself regarding US/Canada). While the integration of a tracking app and the specific focus on Buenos Aires adds local context, the fundamental mechanism is not novel. The proposal does not introduce a new technology or a fundamentally different behavioral intervention model compared to existing 'Earth Hour' or 'Car-Free Day' initiatives.

impact.rationale: The short-term impact is limited to a single day of reduced emissions (approx. 66 tCO2e), which is negligible in the context of city-wide or global emissions. The long-term impact projections (e.g., 39 million tCO2e/year globally) are based on highly speculative extrapolations of behavioral change (e.g., assuming a one-day event leads to weekly home-office adoption and a 10% increase in recycling). The causal link between a one-off awareness event and systemic infrastructure changes (like electric bus implementation) is weak and lacks a credible implementation pathway.

presentation.rationale: The proposal is structured with clear sections and attempts to use data, but the presentation of the impact calculations is confusing and mathematically inconsistent (e.g., the jump from 1% solar coverage to 70,000 tCO2e savings seems disconnected from the event's direct influence). The business model canvas and references to external images (which are not visible) suggest a reliance on visual aids that may not be fully explained in the text. The tone is enthusiastic but lacks the rigorous operational detail expected for a scalable project.

A3.perspective_summary: From an implementation standpoint, this proposal is a classic 'awareness event' rather than a scalable operational solution. It faces significant barriers in securing the necessary government and corporate partnerships required to execute a city-wide shutdown of car usage. The financial model is vague, relying on uncertain sponsorships and government support without a clear cost-recovery or sustainability plan. While the health and environmental goals are noble, the operational feasibility of coordinating 5% of a dense city's population for a single day is low without a pre-existing, robust infrastructure of incentives and safety protocols.
key_concerns: Dependency on government authorization and corporate policy changes (home-office) which are high-friction, slow-moving bureaucratic processes.
Lack of a defined budget or funding source for the significant operational costs (security, medical, media, app development).
The 'Google' app dependency is a critical single point of failure; no alternative technical strategy is presented.
Safety and liability risks for pedestrians and cyclists in a dense urban environment without a detailed traffic management plan.
The long-term impact projections assume a causal link between a one-day event and systemic policy changes (e.g., electric bus rollout) that is not supported by the proposal's logic.

feasibility.rationale: The proposal assumes a level of government and corporate cooperation that is rarely granted for a single-day event without a pilot phase. Specifically, the requirement for the government to 'arrange with the transportation about the modification of their routes' and for companies to universally allow 'home office' on a specific day is operationally unrealistic. Furthermore, the reliance on a custom Google-developed app to track specific GHG savings for a one-off event ignores the high barrier to entry for app adoption and data privacy concerns among the general public.

novelty.rationale: The core concept of 'Walk to Work' or 'Bike to Work' days is a well-established global practice (as the proposal itself admits by citing US/Canada examples). While the specific context of Buenos Aires and the integration of 'Ecobici' adds local flavor, the mechanism of using a smartphone app to gamify a single day of walking is not novel. The proposal does not introduce a new method of engagement or a unique technological solution to the problem of sedentary lifestyles or emissions.

impact.rationale: The projected long-term impacts are highly speculative and likely inflated. The proposal forecasts a 10% increase in recycling and a 5% city-wide solar coverage as direct results of a single walking day, which lacks a logical causal link. The short-term impact of 66 tCO2e is negligible in the context of a city of 3 million people. The proposal fails to address the 'rebound effect' or the reality that a one-day event rarely translates into permanent habit change without structural support (e.g., permanent infrastructure, policy changes) which is not guaranteed here.

presentation.rationale: The proposal is structured with clear sections and attempts to use data, but the tone is overly optimistic and lacks critical self-reflection on barriers. The inclusion of image placeholders (e.g., 'main-objections.png') without the actual content in the text makes the argument incomplete. The calculations for long-term impact are presented as facts rather than hypotheses, which undermines credibility from a community perspective that values realistic expectations.

A4.perspective_summary: From the ground, this proposal feels like a top-down campaign that underestimates the daily friction people face when trying to walk or bike in a dense, car-centric city like Buenos Aires. While the intent to promote health and awareness is positive, the plan relies on idealized scenarios where everyone can simply choose to walk or work from home, ignoring those with mobility issues, long commutes, or jobs that require physical presence. The 'big data' promise feels like a distraction from the lack of concrete, immediate support for the most vulnerable participants who would be walking in unsafe or disconnected neighborhoods.
key_concerns: Accessibility: The proposal assumes everyone can walk 10-15 blocks or bike, ignoring people with disabilities, the elderly, parents with strollers, or those living in areas with poor sidewalk infrastructure.
Safety: There is no mention of safety measures for pedestrians and cyclists on days with reduced but still present traffic, nor for those walking in high-crime or poorly lit areas.

Equity: The 'home office' incentive excludes low-income workers in service, manufacturing, or care sectors who cannot work remotely, potentially making the event feel exclusive to white-collar workers.

Data Privacy: The proposal asks participants to share location data and commute habits with a Google-developed app without addressing how this sensitive data will be protected or if users trust the government/Google with it.
Unintended Consequences: If public transport routes are modified or if traffic patterns shift unpredictably, it could disrupt essential services for those who rely on them daily.

feasibility.rationale: The proposal relies on a highly optimistic and unproven chain of causality: a single-day walking event is expected to trigger systemic policy changes (e.g., 2% electric bus implementation, 10% recycling increase) and long-term behavioral shifts without a concrete mechanism for enforcement or sustained engagement. The assumption that the government will automatically re-route transportation or that companies will adopt home-office policies based on a one-off event is speculative and ignores significant institutional inertia.

novelty.rationale: The core concept of 'Walk to Work' or 'Bike to Work' days is a well-established global practice, not a novel innovation. While the proposal attempts to add value through a smartphone app for tracking and data aggregation, the underlying behavioral intervention is standard. The novelty is further diluted by the reliance on existing 'ecobici' programs and generic 'ecological footprint' algorithms rather than a new methodological approach to climate action.

impact.rationale: There is a severe disconnect between the short-term, symbolic impact of a one-day event and the massive long-term impact figures projected (e.g., 39 million tCO2e/year globally). The proposal hides a critical value conflict: it prioritizes 'awareness' and 'data collection' over tangible, immediate emission reductions. Furthermore, the impact assessment excludes the most vulnerable populations who cannot walk or bike due to distance, disability, or safety concerns, effectively shifting the burden of climate action onto those with the physical capacity to do so while ignoring structural barriers.

presentation.rationale: The proposal is structured with clear sections and attempts to use data, but the presentation of impact calculations is misleading. It presents speculative long-term policy outcomes (solar law approval, electric bus rollout) as direct consequences of the event, obscuring the lack of causal evidence. The text also contains grammatical errors and inconsistent formatting (e.g., mixing 'tCO2e' with 'mtones'), which undermines the credibility of the data presented.

A5.perspective_summary: This proposal frames climate action as a voluntary, individual moral choice ('altruistic spirit') while obscuring the structural inequalities that prevent many from participating. It risks creating a 'participation gap' where only the able-bodied and those living near work centers benefit from the 'awareness' and potential prizes, while the costs of reduced public transport efficiency or safety risks for walkers are not adequately addressed. The projection of massive long-term impacts from a single-day event is ethically problematic as it may divert resources from more robust, systemic interventions.
key_concerns: Exclusion risk: The proposal assumes all participants can walk or bike, ignoring those with disabilities, chronic illnesses, or those living in areas with unsafe infrastructure.

Value conflict: The proposal conflates 'awareness' with 'action,' potentially creating a false sense of accomplishment without addressing the root causes of high emissions (e.g., urban sprawl, lack of affordable public transit).

Distributional consequences: The 'prizes' and 'benefits' are likely to accrue to those already privileged enough to participate, while the 'costs' (e.g., traffic congestion for non-participants, safety risks) are borne by the broader community.
Causal overreach: The proposal attributes complex, long-term policy changes (electric bus rollout, solar law) to a single-day event without a clear governance or advocacy strategy to bridge that gap.

feasibility.rationale: The proposal relies on a highly optimistic and unproven causal chain where a single-day awareness event triggers systemic policy shifts (e.g., 2% electric bus adoption, 1% solar coverage) and behavioral changes (10% recycling increase) over five years. The assumption that a one-off 'Walk Day' will compel the government to restructure public transport routes or pass specific energy laws lacks a realistic implementation pathway. Furthermore, the logistical requirement for 5% of a dense city's population to simultaneously walk/bike while the government and media coordinate real-time data tracking presents significant operational fragility.

novelty.rationale: The core concept of 'Walk to Work' or 'Bike to Work' days is a well-established global practice, not a novel intervention. While the proposal attempts to integrate a smartphone app for data tracking and links it to 'Big Data' for urban planning, the fundamental mechanism remains a standard awareness campaign. The novelty is superficial, relying on the aggregation of existing ideas rather than a new systemic approach to decarbonization or resilience.

impact.rationale: The short-term impact is negligible (66 tCO2e/day) and transient, likely resulting in a 'rebound effect' where participants return to car usage immediately after the event. The long-term impact projections are speculative and mathematically disconnected from the intervention; attributing 105,000+ tCO2e/year in savings to a single awareness day ignores the complex, multi-decadal nature of infrastructure change (e.g., solar law approval, electric bus fleet turnover). The proposal risks shifting the burden of climate action to individual behavior without addressing the systemic drivers of emissions, potentially creating 'awareness fatigue' rather than genuine resilience.

presentation.rationale: The proposal is structured with clear sections and attempts to use data to support claims. However, the presentation suffers from significant logical gaps and calculation errors (e.g., the extrapolation of local savings to global figures assumes a uniform global population density and behavior that does not exist). The reliance on image placeholders (e.g., 'main-objections.png') without textual explanation weakens the argument. The tone is optimistic but lacks the rigorous systems thinking required to justify the massive long-term claims made.

A6.perspective_summary: From a long-term sustainability lens, this proposal functions more as a symbolic gesture than a systemic solution. While it correctly identifies the need for behavioral change and health benefits, it fails to account for the inertia of urban systems and the likelihood of problem-shifting (e.g., increased congestion on remaining transit lines, safety risks for walkers in non-walkable infrastructure). The projected long-term impacts are not causally linked to the proposed activity, risking a disconnect between the event's scale and the magnitude of the climate crisis.
key_concerns: The causal link between a one-day event and long-term infrastructure policy changes (solar laws, electric bus fleets) is unproven and likely non-existent without a dedicated, multi-year advocacy strategy.
The proposal assumes a 'safety in numbers' effect for walkers/cyclists without addressing the immediate physical safety risks in a city not currently designed for high-volume active transport.
There is a risk of 'greenwashing' where the event creates a perception of action while actual systemic emissions remain unchanged or increase due to rebound effects.
The data collection model relies on voluntary participation and app usage, which may introduce significant bias and fail to provide the 'Big Data' insights claimed for urban planning.

The proposal does not address the equity implications: low-income populations often rely on public transit or walking out of necessity, not choice, and may be excluded from the 'prize' incentives or unable to participate if they live too far from work.

A1.text: The collective analysis from peers A2 through A6 robustly validates my initial assessment of the proposal's scientific and systemic fragility. Specifically, A4 and A5's focus on equity and accessibility adds a critical dimension to my technical critique: the proposal's assumption that 5% of the population can simply 'walk' ignores the structural reality of Buenos Aires' infrastructure and the exclusion of vulnerable groups (elderly, disabled, low-income workers). This confirms that the 'causal mechanism' for habit formation is not just unproven but potentially inequitable. Furthermore, A6's point on the 'rebound effect' and A2's emphasis on the lack of governance levers for policy change (solar laws, bus routes) reinforce my view that the long-term impact projections are scientifically unsound. The consensus on the speculative nature of the 'Google app' dependency and the logical leap from a one-day event to systemic policy shifts is unanimous. While the proposal's intent is positive, the evidence base for its claimed outcomes remains non-existent. I maintain my original scores as the pool confirms, rather than revises, the severity of the feasibility and impact gaps.

A2.text: The collective analysis from peers A1, A3, A4, A5, and A6 powerfully reinforces my initial governance assessment. Specifically, A4's focus on equity and accessibility (excluding those with disabilities or long commutes) and A5's critique of the 'participation gap' add critical social governance dimensions I had not fully emphasized. These perspectives confirm that the proposal's reliance on voluntary individual action without structural safeguards creates significant political and social risks, not just operational ones. Furthermore, A1 and A6's detailed breakdown of the 'causal overreach' regarding long-term policy outcomes (solar laws, electric buses) validates my concern that the proposal lacks a realistic institutional pathway to translate a one-day event into systemic change. The consensus on the unfeasibility of government-mandated route modifications and corporate home-office shifts for a single day is absolute. While the 'Big Data' concept has theoretical merit, the lack of a privacy framework (noted by A4) and the speculative nature of the data utility further weaken the governance case. My scores remain unchanged as the pool confirms the proposal's fundamental disconnect between its ambitious policy goals and its weak institutional levers.

A3.text: The pool strongly validates my initial operational concerns regarding the 'Google-developed app' dependency and the unrealistic assumption of government route modifications for a single day. A4 and A5 add critical depth by highlighting the equity and safety barriers (accessibility, crime, infrastructure) that my feasibility score flagged but did not fully detail. A6's point on 'rebound effects' and 'awareness fatigue' further qualifies the long-term impact claims. While I maintain that the core concept is a low-feasibility, high-risk 'awareness event' rather than a scalable solution, the consensus on the lack of a concrete implementation pathway for the long-term policy goals (solar laws, electric buses) confirms that the impact projections are not just optimistic but operationally ungrounded. No score revision is needed as the pool reinforces my initial assessment of high risk and low feasibility.

A4.text: Reading the pool, I see strong alignment with my concerns regarding accessibility and equity. A1, A2, and A5 correctly highlight the 'causal overreach' in linking a single event to complex policy shifts (solar laws, bus fleets), which I noted as a lack of structural support. A5's point about the 'participation gap' is crucial: the proposal's focus on 'prizes' and 'home office' implicitly excludes low-income workers and those with disabilities, turning a climate initiative into an exclusive event for the privileged. A6's warning about 'greenwashing' and the 'rebound effect' reinforces my view that without addressing safety and infrastructure *before* the event, we risk creating a false sense of accomplishment. While the 'Big Data' angle (A2, A3) has potential for urban planning, the current proposal treats data collection as a substitute for the hard work of making streets safe for the most vulnerable. I maintain my scores as the fundamental disconnect between the proposal's top-down optimism and the ground-level reality of exclusion and safety risks remains unaddressed.

A5.text: The pool strongly validates my initial concerns regarding exclusion and the 'moralization' of climate action. A4's specific focus on accessibility (disabilities, elderly, safety in poor neighborhoods) and A2's point on the exclusion of low-income workers who cannot work from home or afford to skip public transport deepen the ethical critique: this proposal risks becoming a privilege-based performance rather than a just transition. While A1, A3, and A6 correctly identify the causal overreach and operational fragility, they complement my view by showing *why* the exclusion happens—the proposal relies on a 'one-size-fits-all' behavioral model that ignores structural barriers. I maintain my scores; the consensus confirms that the proposal's 'awareness' value is heavily offset by significant distributional costs borne by the most vulnerable, and the projected impacts remain ethically unsound due to this lack of inclusivity.

A6.text: The collective analysis from A1-A5 powerfully reinforces my initial assessment that the proposal's causal logic is the primary failure point. Specifically, A4's focus on equity and accessibility adds a critical dimension to my 'resilience' critique: a system that relies on voluntary walking without addressing infrastructure safety or the exclusion of low-income/remote workers is not resilient; it risks deepening social divides under the guise of climate action. A2 and A5's emphasis on the lack of institutional levers confirms that the proposed 'awareness' mechanism cannot sustain the systemic shifts (solar laws, bus electrification) it claims to trigger. While I maintain that the long-term impact scores must remain low due to this speculative causality, the pool's detailed breakdown of operational fragility (A1, A3) and the 'rebound effect' risks (A4, A5) solidifies the view that this is a symbolic gesture rather than a structural solution. No score revision is needed as the pool's inputs validate the severity of the initial concerns rather than mitigating them.

feasibility.rationale: The proposal relies on a highly optimistic and unverified assumption that 5% of the city's population (approx. 145,000 people) will voluntarily participate in a single-day event without significant logistical friction. Furthermore, the technical feasibility of the proposed 'Google-developed' app is speculative; the proposal assumes a major tech corporation will build custom infrastructure for a one-off event without a clear business case or prior commitment. The requirement for government authorization to 'modify bus routes' for a single day is operationally complex and unlikely to be approved for a temporary awareness event.

novelty.rationale: The core concept—a 'Walk to Work' or 'Bike to Work' day—is a well-established global practice (e.g., Earth Hour, Bike to Work Day) with extensive existing literature. While the proposal attempts to integrate a specific data-tracking app and local context (Buenos Aires), the fundamental mechanism of action is not novel. The proposed algorithm for calculating footprints is explicitly stated to be a replication of existing government tools, offering no new scientific or technical methodology.

impact.rationale: The short-term impact calculation (66.46 tCO2e/day) is mathematically plausible for a single day but represents a negligible fraction of annual emissions. The long-term impact projections are scientifically unsound due to a lack of causal evidence. The proposal asserts a direct causal link between a one-day walking event and a 10% increase in recycling, the approval of solar laws, and the implementation of electric buses. These are systemic policy outcomes that cannot be reliably attributed to a single behavioral awareness event; the correlation is assumed without empirical support, leading to a massive overestimation of impact (e.g., projecting 39 million tCO2e/year globally from a single city's pilot).

presentation.rationale: The presentation suffers from significant structural and logical inconsistencies. The text oscillates between a 'practice' and a 'project' without clarity. The impact section contains severe logical leaps, attributing long-term infrastructure and policy changes (solar laws, electric bus fleets) directly to the event's awareness campaign. The reliance on hypothetical partnerships (Google, MIT Colab) and the assumption that the government will modify public transport routes for a single day undermines the credibility of the technical and operational narrative.

A1.perspective_summary: From a systems and empirical evidence perspective, this proposal conflates a short-term behavioral nudge with long-term systemic policy shifts. While the immediate emission savings from a single day of reduced car use are calculable, the causal chain linking this event to major infrastructure changes (solar laws, electric bus adoption) and global scaling is scientifically unsupported. The proposal lacks a robust mechanism for the claimed long-term habit formation and relies on speculative partnerships for its technical execution.
key_concerns: Causal Overreach: The proposal assumes a one-day event will directly cause complex policy outcomes (solar law approval, electric bus implementation) without evidence of such a causal mechanism.
Data Integrity: The methodology for tracking and verifying GHG savings relies on a hypothetical app and unverified user self-reporting, introducing high uncertainty into the impact metrics.

Operational Feasibility: The assumption that 5% of the population will participate and that public transport routes will be modified for a single day is operationally unrealistic without a detailed implementation plan.
Scaling Logic: The extrapolation from a single city event to global impact (39 million tCO2e/year) ignores the diminishing returns and contextual differences of scaling behavioral interventions.
Dependency Risk: The proposal's technical execution is contingent on the voluntary cooperation of major entities (Google, Government) that have not been secured.

feasibility.rationale: The proposal relies on unrealistic institutional levers, specifically the assumption that the government can mandate companies to allow 'home office' and that transportation authorities will modify routes based on a single-day event. The governance model assumes voluntary participation will yield 5% of the city's population (approx. 145,000 people) without a clear enforcement or incentive mechanism beyond vague 'prizes.' Furthermore, the reliance on a Google-developed app and the assumption that the government will 'notify' companies of the proposal ignores the complex regulatory and labor frameworks required to shift work habits at scale.

novelty.rationale: The concept of 'Walk to Work' or 'Bike to Work' days is a well-established global practice (e.g., Earth Hour, Bike to Work Day in the US/Canada). The proposal's novelty lies primarily in the specific integration of a tracking app to generate 'big data' for urban planning and the attempt to link this data to long-term policy outcomes (solar law, electric buses). However, the core mechanism is not innovative, and the proposal does not address how this specific iteration differs significantly from existing, often more mature, municipal programs.

impact.rationale: While the short-term emission savings (66 tCO2e/day) are mathematically derived, the long-term impact projections are highly speculative and lack a causal governance link. The proposal forecasts a 10% increase in recycling and the approval of a distributed solar law as direct results of a single walking event, which is a significant overestimation of behavioral spillover. The governance capacity to translate a one-day awareness event into structural changes like '2% electric bus implementation' or 'solar law approval' is not demonstrated, making the long-term impact claims unrealistic.

presentation.rationale: The proposal is structured with clear sections (Purpose, Actions, Impacts, Costs) and attempts to use data to support claims. However, the presentation suffers from logical gaps in the policy argumentation, such as the jump from a walking event to the approval of specific legislation. The text also contains grammatical errors and inconsistent formatting (e.g., image placeholders like 'main-objections.png' without context), which undermines the professional credibility required for a governance-focused proposal.

A2.perspective_summary: From a governance and regulatory perspective, this proposal is an awareness campaign masquerading as a policy intervention. It lacks the institutional depth to enforce the behavioral changes it predicts and relies on optimistic assumptions about government and corporate cooperation that are not grounded in current regulatory realities. While the data collection idea has merit, the proposal fails to outline the necessary political or administrative levers to achieve its ambitious long-term policy goals.
key_concerns: The proposal assumes the government can mandate or strongly influence corporate 'home office' policies without a legal framework or labor agreement.
There is no clear mechanism for how a one-day event translates into long-term legislative outcomes (e.g., solar law approval, electric bus adoption).
The reliance on a single-day event to shift deep-seated commuting habits ignores the need for sustained infrastructure investment and policy continuity.
The data privacy and governance implications of a government-linked app tracking citizen movement are not addressed.
The assumption that 5% of the population will voluntarily participate without significant incentives or mandates is unproven.

feasibility.rationale: The proposal relies on a 'big bang' single-day event model with high coordination costs (security, health, media) but lacks a clear operational plan for the critical dependency on government authorization and corporate buy-in for home-office policies. The assumption that 5% of the population will voluntarily walk to work on a specific day without a robust incentive structure or logistical support (e.g., traffic management, safety) is operationally optimistic. Furthermore, the reliance on a custom app developed by 'Google if possible' without a defined technical partner or budget creates a significant execution risk.

novelty.rationale: The core concept of 'Walk to Work' or 'Bike to Work' days is a well-established global practice (referenced in the proposal itself regarding US/Canada). While the integration of a tracking app and the specific focus on Buenos Aires adds local context, the fundamental mechanism is not novel. The proposal does not introduce a new technology or a fundamentally different behavioral intervention model compared to existing 'Earth Hour' or 'Car-Free Day' initiatives.

impact.rationale: The short-term impact is limited to a single day of reduced emissions (approx. 66 tCO2e), which is negligible in the context of city-wide or global emissions. The long-term impact projections (e.g., 39 million tCO2e/year globally) are based on highly speculative extrapolations of behavioral change (e.g., assuming a one-day event leads to weekly home-office adoption and a 10% increase in recycling). The causal link between a one-off awareness event and systemic infrastructure changes (like electric bus implementation) is weak and lacks a credible implementation pathway.

presentation.rationale: The proposal is structured with clear sections and attempts to use data, but the presentation of the impact calculations is confusing and mathematically inconsistent (e.g., the jump from 1% solar coverage to 70,000 tCO2e savings seems disconnected from the event's direct influence). The business model canvas and references to external images (which are not visible) suggest a reliance on visual aids that may not be fully explained in the text. The tone is enthusiastic but lacks the rigorous operational detail expected for a scalable project.

A3.perspective_summary: From an implementation standpoint, this proposal is a classic 'awareness event' rather than a scalable operational solution. It faces significant barriers in securing the necessary government and corporate partnerships required to execute a city-wide shutdown of car usage. The financial model is vague, relying on uncertain sponsorships and government support without a clear cost-recovery or sustainability plan. While the health and environmental goals are noble, the operational feasibility of coordinating 5% of a dense city's population for a single day is low without a pre-existing, robust infrastructure of incentives and safety protocols.
key_concerns: Dependency on government authorization and corporate policy changes (home-office) which are high-friction, slow-moving bureaucratic processes.
Lack of a defined budget or funding source for the significant operational costs (security, medical, media, app development).
The 'Google' app dependency is a critical single point of failure; no alternative technical strategy is presented.
Safety and liability risks for pedestrians and cyclists in a dense urban environment without a detailed traffic management plan.
The long-term impact projections assume a causal link between a one-day event and systemic policy changes (e.g., electric bus rollout) that is not supported by the proposal's logic.

feasibility.rationale: The proposal assumes a level of government and corporate cooperation that is rarely granted for a single-day event without a pilot phase. Specifically, the requirement for the government to 'arrange with the transportation about the modification of their routes' and for companies to universally allow 'home office' on a specific day is operationally unrealistic. Furthermore, the reliance on a custom Google-developed app to track specific GHG savings for a one-off event ignores the high barrier to entry for app adoption and data privacy concerns among the general public.

novelty.rationale: The core concept of 'Walk to Work' or 'Bike to Work' days is a well-established global practice (as the proposal itself admits by citing US/Canada examples). While the specific context of Buenos Aires and the integration of 'Ecobici' adds local flavor, the mechanism of using a smartphone app to gamify a single day of walking is not novel. The proposal does not introduce a new method of engagement or a unique technological solution to the problem of sedentary lifestyles or emissions.

impact.rationale: The projected long-term impacts are highly speculative and likely inflated. The proposal forecasts a 10% increase in recycling and a 5% city-wide solar coverage as direct results of a single walking day, which lacks a logical causal link. The short-term impact of 66 tCO2e is negligible in the context of a city of 3 million people. The proposal fails to address the 'rebound effect' or the reality that a one-day event rarely translates into permanent habit change without structural support (e.g., permanent infrastructure, policy changes) which is not guaranteed here.

presentation.rationale: The proposal is structured with clear sections and attempts to use data, but the tone is overly optimistic and lacks critical self-reflection on barriers. The inclusion of image placeholders (e.g., 'main-objections.png') without the actual content in the text makes the argument incomplete. The calculations for long-term impact are presented as facts rather than hypotheses, which undermines credibility from a community perspective that values realistic expectations.

A4.perspective_summary: From the ground, this proposal feels like a top-down campaign that underestimates the daily friction people face when trying to walk or bike in a dense, car-centric city like Buenos Aires. While the intent to promote health and awareness is positive, the plan relies on idealized scenarios where everyone can simply choose to walk or work from home, ignoring those with mobility issues, long commutes, or jobs that require physical presence. The 'big data' promise feels like a distraction from the lack of concrete, immediate support for the most vulnerable participants who would be walking in unsafe or disconnected neighborhoods.
key_concerns: Accessibility: The proposal assumes everyone can walk 10-15 blocks or bike, ignoring people with disabilities, the elderly, parents with strollers, or those living in areas with poor sidewalk infrastructure.
Safety: There is no mention of safety measures for pedestrians and cyclists on days with reduced but still present traffic, nor for those walking in high-crime or poorly lit areas.

Equity: The 'home office' incentive excludes low-income workers in service, manufacturing, or care sectors who cannot work remotely, potentially making the event feel exclusive to white-collar workers.

Data Privacy: The proposal asks participants to share location data and commute habits with a Google-developed app without addressing how this sensitive data will be protected or if users trust the government/Google with it.
Unintended Consequences: If public transport routes are modified or if traffic patterns shift unpredictably, it could disrupt essential services for those who rely on them daily.

feasibility.rationale: The proposal relies on a highly optimistic and unproven chain of causality: a single-day walking event is expected to trigger systemic policy changes (e.g., 2% electric bus implementation, 10% recycling increase) and long-term behavioral shifts without a concrete mechanism for enforcement or sustained engagement. The assumption that the government will automatically re-route transportation or that companies will adopt home-office policies based on a one-off event is speculative and ignores significant institutional inertia.

novelty.rationale: The core concept of 'Walk to Work' or 'Bike to Work' days is a well-established global practice, not a novel innovation. While the proposal attempts to add value through a smartphone app for tracking and data aggregation, the underlying behavioral intervention is standard. The novelty is further diluted by the reliance on existing 'ecobici' programs and generic 'ecological footprint' algorithms rather than a new methodological approach to climate action.

impact.rationale: There is a severe disconnect between the short-term, symbolic impact of a one-day event and the massive long-term impact figures projected (e.g., 39 million tCO2e/year globally). The proposal hides a critical value conflict: it prioritizes 'awareness' and 'data collection' over tangible, immediate emission reductions. Furthermore, the impact assessment excludes the most vulnerable populations who cannot walk or bike due to distance, disability, or safety concerns, effectively shifting the burden of climate action onto those with the physical capacity to do so while ignoring structural barriers.

presentation.rationale: The proposal is structured with clear sections and attempts to use data, but the presentation of impact calculations is misleading. It presents speculative long-term policy outcomes (solar law approval, electric bus rollout) as direct consequences of the event, obscuring the lack of causal evidence. The text also contains grammatical errors and inconsistent formatting (e.g., mixing 'tCO2e' with 'mtones'), which undermines the credibility of the data presented.

A5.perspective_summary: This proposal frames climate action as a voluntary, individual moral choice ('altruistic spirit') while obscuring the structural inequalities that prevent many from participating. It risks creating a 'participation gap' where only the able-bodied and those living near work centers benefit from the 'awareness' and potential prizes, while the costs of reduced public transport efficiency or safety risks for walkers are not adequately addressed. The projection of massive long-term impacts from a single-day event is ethically problematic as it may divert resources from more robust, systemic interventions.
key_concerns: Exclusion risk: The proposal assumes all participants can walk or bike, ignoring those with disabilities, chronic illnesses, or those living in areas with unsafe infrastructure.

Value conflict: The proposal conflates 'awareness' with 'action,' potentially creating a false sense of accomplishment without addressing the root causes of high emissions (e.g., urban sprawl, lack of affordable public transit).

Distributional consequences: The 'prizes' and 'benefits' are likely to accrue to those already privileged enough to participate, while the 'costs' (e.g., traffic congestion for non-participants, safety risks) are borne by the broader community.
Causal overreach: The proposal attributes complex, long-term policy changes (electric bus rollout, solar law) to a single-day event without a clear governance or advocacy strategy to bridge that gap.

feasibility.rationale: The proposal relies on a highly optimistic and unproven causal chain where a single-day awareness event triggers systemic policy shifts (e.g., 2% electric bus adoption, 1% solar coverage) and behavioral changes (10% recycling increase) over five years. The assumption that a one-off 'Walk Day' will compel the government to restructure public transport routes or pass specific energy laws lacks a realistic implementation pathway. Furthermore, the logistical requirement for 5% of a dense city's population to simultaneously walk/bike while the government and media coordinate real-time data tracking presents significant operational fragility.

novelty.rationale: The core concept of 'Walk to Work' or 'Bike to Work' days is a well-established global practice, not a novel intervention. While the proposal attempts to integrate a smartphone app for data tracking and links it to 'Big Data' for urban planning, the fundamental mechanism remains a standard awareness campaign. The novelty is superficial, relying on the aggregation of existing ideas rather than a new systemic approach to decarbonization or resilience.

impact.rationale: The short-term impact is negligible (66 tCO2e/day) and transient, likely resulting in a 'rebound effect' where participants return to car usage immediately after the event. The long-term impact projections are speculative and mathematically disconnected from the intervention; attributing 105,000+ tCO2e/year in savings to a single awareness day ignores the complex, multi-decadal nature of infrastructure change (e.g., solar law approval, electric bus fleet turnover). The proposal risks shifting the burden of climate action to individual behavior without addressing the systemic drivers of emissions, potentially creating 'awareness fatigue' rather than genuine resilience.

presentation.rationale: The proposal is structured with clear sections and attempts to use data to support claims. However, the presentation suffers from significant logical gaps and calculation errors (e.g., the extrapolation of local savings to global figures assumes a uniform global population density and behavior that does not exist). The reliance on image placeholders (e.g., 'main-objections.png') without textual explanation weakens the argument. The tone is optimistic but lacks the rigorous systems thinking required to justify the massive long-term claims made.

A6.perspective_summary: From a long-term sustainability lens, this proposal functions more as a symbolic gesture than a systemic solution. While it correctly identifies the need for behavioral change and health benefits, it fails to account for the inertia of urban systems and the likelihood of problem-shifting (e.g., increased congestion on remaining transit lines, safety risks for walkers in non-walkable infrastructure). The projected long-term impacts are not causally linked to the proposed activity, risking a disconnect between the event's scale and the magnitude of the climate crisis.
key_concerns: The causal link between a one-day event and long-term infrastructure policy changes (solar laws, electric bus fleets) is unproven and likely non-existent without a dedicated, multi-year advocacy strategy.
The proposal assumes a 'safety in numbers' effect for walkers/cyclists without addressing the immediate physical safety risks in a city not currently designed for high-volume active transport.
There is a risk of 'greenwashing' where the event creates a perception of action while actual systemic emissions remain unchanged or increase due to rebound effects.
The data collection model relies on voluntary participation and app usage, which may introduce significant bias and fail to provide the 'Big Data' insights claimed for urban planning.

The proposal does not address the equity implications: low-income populations often rely on public transit or walking out of necessity, not choice, and may be excluded from the 'prize' incentives or unable to participate if they live too far from work.

clusters.label: Causal Overreach and Impact Validity
clusters.summary: All experts unanimously identify a critical logical gap between the proposed single-day awareness event and the massive long-term systemic outcomes claimed (e.g., 10% recycling increase, solar law approval, electric bus implementation). The consensus is that the proposal lacks a credible causal mechanism to link a one-off behavioral nudge to complex policy and infrastructure changes, rendering the long-term impact projections scientifically unsound and speculative.

clusters.label: Operational Feasibility and Institutional Dependencies
clusters.summary: Experts highlight severe operational risks stemming from unrealistic assumptions about institutional cooperation. The proposal relies on unverified commitments from major tech corporations (Google) for app development, government authorization to modify public transport routes for a single day, and corporate mandates for home-office policies. These dependencies are viewed as high-friction bureaucratic hurdles that make the execution of the event highly improbable without a detailed implementation plan.

clusters.label: Equity, Accessibility, and Social Exclusion
clusters.summary: A strong consensus emerges regarding the proposal's failure to account for structural inequalities. Experts note that the 'walk or bike' model inherently excludes vulnerable populations, including those with disabilities, the elderly, low-income workers who cannot work remotely, and residents of unsafe neighborhoods. The proposal is criticized for framing climate action as a voluntary, individual moral choice that privileges the able-bodied and white-collar workers, potentially creating a 'participation gap' and ignoring safety risks.

clusters.label: Novelty and Strategic Redundancy
clusters.summary: Experts agree that the core concept of 'Walk to Work' or 'Bike to Work' days is a well-established global practice with no significant novelty. While the proposal attempts to differentiate itself through data tracking and local context (Buenos Aires), the fundamental mechanism is standard. The reliance on existing tools (Ecobici, government footprint calculators) and the lack of a new technological or behavioral intervention model further diminish its innovative value.

clusters.label: Presentation Quality and Data Integrity
clusters.summary: The proposal's presentation is criticized for logical inconsistencies, grammatical errors, and the use of missing image placeholders. Experts point out that the impact calculations are misleading, presenting speculative long-term policy outcomes as direct facts. The reliance on hypothetical data from a non-existent app and the extrapolation of local savings to global figures without accounting for contextual differences undermines the credibility of the data presented.

open_questions: What specific, evidence-based governance mechanisms could realistically link a single-day awareness event to long-term legislative outcomes like solar law approval or electric bus fleet adoption?

How can the proposal be redesigned to ensure equitable participation for low-income workers, people with disabilities, and those living in areas with unsafe infrastructure, rather than relying on a 'one-size-fits-all' voluntary model?

What is the minimum viable operational plan for securing the necessary government and corporate partnerships (e.g., route modifications, home-office mandates) before the event can be considered feasible?
How can the data collection strategy be validated to ensure privacy and accuracy without relying on speculative partnerships with major tech corporations?
What alternative metrics or intermediate goals could replace the current speculative long-term impact projections to provide a more realistic assessment of the event's value?

feasibility.rationale: The proposal relies on highly speculative institutional dependencies, including a custom app developed by Google, government authorization to modify public transport routes for a single day, and corporate mandates for home-office policies. The assumption that 5% of a dense city's population will voluntarily participate without a robust incentive structure or logistical safety plan is operationally unrealistic. The lack of a concrete implementation pathway for these high-friction bureaucratic hurdles significantly undermines feasibility.

novelty.rationale: The core concept of 'Walk to Work' or 'Bike to Work' days is a well-established global practice with extensive existing literature. While the proposal attempts to integrate a tracking app and local context (Buenos Aires), the fundamental mechanism is not innovative. The proposed algorithm for calculating footprints is explicitly a replication of existing government tools, and the 'Big Data' component does not introduce a new methodological approach to climate action.

impact.rationale: There is a severe disconnect between the short-term, symbolic impact of a one-day event and the massive long-term systemic outcomes claimed. The proposal lacks a credible causal mechanism to link a single-day behavioral nudge to complex policy changes like solar law approval, electric bus fleet adoption, or a 10% increase in recycling. The long-term projections (e.g., 39 million tCO2e/year globally) are scientifically unsound extrapolations that ignore the inertia of urban systems and the likelihood of rebound effects.

presentation.rationale: The presentation suffers from significant structural and logical inconsistencies, including oscillating between 'practice' and 'project' classifications. It relies on missing image placeholders and contains grammatical errors. Most critically, the impact section presents speculative long-term policy outcomes as direct facts without empirical support, and the mathematical extrapolations from local to global scales are flawed, undermining the proposal's credibility.

final.synthesis: The expert consensus is overwhelmingly negative across all dimensions, driven by a shared identification of 'causal overreach' and 'operational fragility.' The synthesis integrates the technical critique (A1, A3) regarding the unverified app and logistical risks with the governance critique (A2, A5) concerning the unrealistic expectation of government and corporate cooperation. Furthermore, the social equity perspective (A4, A5, A6) was integrated to highlight that the proposal's voluntary model inherently excludes vulnerable populations, reinforcing the low feasibility and impact scores. While A2 offered a slightly higher novelty score (3) due to the data integration attempt, the overwhelming consensus on the lack of a new behavioral intervention model led to a consolidated score of 2. The final judgment reflects a proposal that is well-intentioned but fundamentally flawed in its logic, execution plan, and impact claims.
final.uncertainty: The primary uncertainty remains whether the 'awareness' component could, in a highly idealized scenario, serve as a catalyst for a broader, multi-year advocacy campaign that the proposal does not currently outline. Additionally, while the experts agree the current plan is unfeasible, there is a minor divergence on whether the 'Big Data' concept itself holds enough latent value to warrant a pivot in strategy rather than a rejection of the core idea. The specific quantitative impact of the short-term event (66 tCO2e) is mathematically consistent but its real-world verification depends on the hypothetical app's adoption rate, which remains unproven.
